title: Daily Routines
shortSummary: Describe your day, from waking up to going to bed, using everyday Turkish verbs.
culturalNote: A Turkish day often begins with a generous breakfast (kahvaltı), which on weekends becomes a long, social spread of cheese, olives, tomatoes, eggs, jam, and endless tea shared with family. The word kahvaltı literally means 'before coffee', a nod to how central drinks are to the rhythm of the day. Many people drink tea morning, afternoon, and evening, and a mid-afternoon çay break is a cherished ritual at home and at work. Lunch (öğle yemeği) is commonly the main hot meal for office workers, while dinner (akşam yemeği) gathers the household together. Reflexive routines like washing up and getting dressed use special verb forms, and Turks describe their schedule naturally with time expressions such as sabah (morning), öğlen (noon), and akşam (evening) placed before the verb.

keyPhrases.english: I get up early in the morning
I make dinner in the evening

grammar.explanation: Turkish word order is subject-object-verb, and time expressions usually sit near the front, before the verb. Sabah (in the morning), öğlen (at noon), akşam (in the evening), and her gün (every day) describe when, and need no preposition; they simply precede the action.

examples.en: In the evening I read a book.

grammar.commonMistake: Putting the time word after the verb. Turkish prefers sabah kalkıyorum, not kalkıyorum sabah.

grammar.explanation: To show motion toward a place, add the dative ending -e or -a (with vowel harmony) to the destination: işe (to work), eve (to home), okula (to school). It answers the question 'to where?' (nereye?) and pairs with verbs of movement like git- (go) and dön- (return).
grammar.commonMistake: Leaving off the dative ending. It is eve gidiyorum (going to home), not ev gidiyorum.

dialogue.setup: Two friends, Mert and Zeynep, compare their morning routines over the phone.

lines.speaker: Mert
lines.english: Good morning Zeynep! Did you get up early?

lines.speaker: Zeynep
lines.english: Yes, I get up at six every day.

lines.speaker: Mert
lines.english: Very early! Do you have breakfast?

lines.speaker: Zeynep
lines.native: Tabii, çay içiyorum ve sonra işe gidiyorum.
lines.english: Of course, I drink tea and then go to work.

lines.speaker: Mert
lines.native: Ben öğlen yemek yiyorum ama kahvaltı yapmıyorum.
lines.english: I eat lunch at noon but I don't have breakfast.

lines.speaker: Zeynep
lines.native: Bu iyi değil! Akşam ne yapıyorsun?
lines.english: That's not good! What do you do in the evening?

lines.speaker: Mert
lines.english: I watch television and go to bed early.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I go to work' in Turkish?
quiz.explanation: İşe uses the dative 'to', and gidiyorum is 'I am going'.

quiz.question: Ev_____ dönüyorum. (I'm returning home)
quiz.explanation: The dative -e on ev gives eve, 'to home'.

quiz.question: Arrange: kalkıyorum / sabah / erken
quiz.explanation: Time words first: 'In the morning early I get up'.
